exercises.title: A: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: You should eat less sugar if you're on a
items.originalSentence: You should eat less sugar if you're on a **diet / cure**.
items.completedSentence: You should eat less sugar if you're on a diet .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "diet" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "diet" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "cure" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.cure: "cure" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.cure: "cure" doesn't fit here → "diet" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

items.sentenceAfter: the onions carefully before cooking them.
items.originalSentence: **Chew / Chop** the onions carefully before cooking them.
items.completedSentence: chop the onions carefully before cooking them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "chop" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "chop" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Chew" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.Chew: "Chew" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the onions carefully before cooking them.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.Chew: "Chew" doesn't fit here → "chop" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the onions carefully before cooking them.").
optionContrast.Chop: "Chop" doesn't fit here → "chop" does, because Only "chop" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: do we need for the soup?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "ingredients" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "ingredients" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "infections" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.Slice: "Slice" doesn't fit here → "stir" does, because Only "stir" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: This yoghurt tastes too **sour / spicy** to eat.

exercises.title: B: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Her skin is very ...... (SENSE), so she uses special cream.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from SENSE, so the correct answer is "sensitive".

wrongOptions.sense: The root SENSE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "sensitive".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SENSE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SENSE → sensitive: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "sensitive" is the word-formation answer from the root SENSE in this context.

items.sentence: Would you like to go ...... (JOG) this evening?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is activity from JOG, so the correct answer is "jogging".

wrongOptions.jog: The root JOG cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "jogging".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of JOG that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: JOG → jogging: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "jogging" is the word-formation answer from the root JOG in this context.

items.sentence: That injection was really ...... (PAIN).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from PAIN, so the correct answer is "painful".

wrongOptions.pain: The root PAIN cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "painful".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PAIN that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PAIN → painful: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "painful" is the word-formation answer from the root PAIN in this context.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "medical" is the word-formation answer from the root MEDICINE in this context.

exercises.title: C: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
I've gained weight recently. **on**
I've ...... weight recently.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is gain weight, so the correct answer is "put on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
You should eat less junk food. **down**
You should ...... junk food.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eat less, so the correct answer is "cut down on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "cut down on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
This cheese isn't fresh anymore. **gone**
This cheese ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not fresh, so the correct answer is "has gone off".

wrongOptions.gone off: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has gone off", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Seafood causes me allergic reactions. **allergic**
I ...... seafood.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is allergy, so the correct answer is "am allergic to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "am allergic to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I hope he recovers from the flu quickly. **over**
I hope he ...... the flu quickly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is recover, so the correct answer is "gets over".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "gets over", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentenceBefore: If you don't drink enough water, you

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is zero conditional, so the correct answer is "get".

wrongOptions.B: "would get" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.
wrongOptions.would get: "would get" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.
wrongOptions.C: "got" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.
wrongOptions.got: "got" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.
wrongOptions.D: "had got" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.
wrongOptions.had got: "had got" belongs to a different conditional pattern — zero conditional needs present forms.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Name the conditional type first — that tells you which verb forms belong in each clause.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Zero conditional: if + present simple, present simple — both clauses describe general truths or habits.

exercises.title: E: Complete the sentences
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: She exercises regularly to ...... healthy.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is remain, so the correct answer is "stay".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: You should ...... more vegetables in your diet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is add, so the correct answer is "include".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Doctors say we should ...... plenty of water every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is hydration, so the correct answer is "drink".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "up" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "Always warm ...... before doing exercise.".
